perturbation.original_text: Ciufo hereby agrees to the jurisdiction of all federal and state courts within the state of the defending party in connection with any matter relating to this Agreement. Ciufo further agrees that service of process may be made, in addition to all other methods permitted by law, by Certified Mail, Return Receipt Requested, sent to the address set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: Ciufo hereby agrees that service of process may be made, in addition to all other methods permitted by law, by Certified Mail, Return Receipt Requested, sent to the address set forth below. Ciufo agrees to the jurisdiction of federal courts only, regardless of the defending party's location, in connection with any matter relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Jurisdiction and service of process clauses are typically grouped together in a 'Governing Law and Jurisdiction' section. By separating and altering the jurisdiction clause, and placing it separately, it could cause confusion about applicable law, and the agreement to only federal courts may conflict with state laws depending on the nature of the dispute.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text limits jurisdiction to federal courts only. However, 28 U.S.C. § 1332 outlines specific requirements for diversity jurisdiction in federal courts, including a minimum amount in controversy and complete diversity of citizenship between the parties. If these requirements are not met, the federal court may lack subject matter jurisdiction, potentially contradicting the agreement's intent to have all disputes resolved in federal court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits jurisdiction to federal courts only. This contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1332, as highlighted in both law snippets, which outlines the requirements for federal court jurisdiction based on diversity of citizenship and a minimum amount in controversy ($75,000). If the parties lack diversity of citizenship or the amount in controversy is less than $75,000, the federal court would lack jurisdiction, making the agreement's attempt to limit jurisdiction solely to federal courts contradictory to established jurisdictional requirements. The original text had a broader approach that would include state jurisdiction, where the modified text does not.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices to the parties pursuant to the terms of this Agreement shall be hand delivered or mailed by Certified Mail, Return Receipt Requested, or overnight courier as follows: If to Ciufo: John Ciufo 20750 Vincent Drive Brookfield, WI 53045 Telephone Number - 414-797-8593 If to Health Card, at: National Medical Health Card Systems, Inc. 26 Harbor Park Drive Port Washington, NY 11050 Attention: Bert E. Brodsky, President Telephone Number: (516) 484-4400 Telecopier Number: (516) 484-6084
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices regarding marketing and promotional materials related to the Project shall be hand delivered or mailed by Certified Mail, Return Receipt Requested, or overnight courier as follows: If to Ciufo: John Ciufo 20750 Vincent Drive Brookfield, WI 53045 Telephone Number - 414-797-8593 If to Health Card, at: National Medical Health Card Systems, Inc. 26 Harbor Park Drive Port Washington, NY 11050 Attention: Bert E. Brodsky, President Telephone Number: (516) 484-4400 Telecopier Number: (516) 484-6084
perturbation.explanation: Moving the general notice provision to a section specifically addressing marketing introduces ambiguity. It suggests that formal legal notices (e.g., breach of contract, termination) may not be valid if sent via these methods, potentially conflicting with standard legal practices for contract enforcement, and applicable NY state law regarding contract interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. CPLR § 2103(b) outlines the methods for serving papers in legal proceedings, including service upon an attorney. By restricting the notice provision to 'marketing and promotional materials,' the modified agreement could be interpreted as excluding legally required notices from these methods, potentially conflicting with the requirements for proper service under New York law.

(b) Upon an attorney. Except where otherwise prescribed by law or order of court, papers to be served upon a party in a pending
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the notice provision to 'marketing and promotional materials,' whereas the original covered 'any notices'. N.Y. CPLR § 2103(b), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, broadly addresses 'Service of papers' in legal proceedings. By restricting the scope of the notice provision, the changed text creates a structural flaw that could prevent proper legal service, leading to non-compliance with New York law, particularly concerning notifications beyond marketing.

perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary Information furnished by Health Card to Ciufo is considered loaned for use solely in connection with the Project, and shall be returned by Ciufo to Health Card upon request by Health Card. Ciufo shall certify that it has destroyed or returned all copies of the Proprietary Information in its possession.
perturbation.changed_text: All office supplies furnished by Health Card to Ciufo are considered loaned for use solely in connection with the Project, and shall be returned by Ciufo to Health Card upon request by Health Card. Ciufo shall certify that it has destroyed or returned all copies of the Proprietary Information in its possession.
perturbation.explanation: The clause requiring the return of proprietary information is shifted to the section regarding general information. By changing "proprietary information" to "office supplies", it diminishes the visibility of the important requirement to return confidential information, weakening the protection of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. § 1836) protects trade secrets. By obscuring the return of proprietary information as a requirement, this modification weakens the agreement's ability to enforce the protection of Health Card's trade secrets, making it more difficult to pursue legal action under the DTSA if Ciufo misappropriates the information. The secondary return requirement makes it seem like the proprietary information may be office supplies.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Proprietary Information' with 'office supplies,' which structurally weakens the contract's protection of trade secrets as governed by the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. § 1836). The original text explicitly requires the return of 'Proprietary Information,' while the changed text diminishes this requirement by associating the return obligation with 'office supplies.' This change misplaces a crucial legal disclosure related to trade secrets into an irrelevant section, making it difficult to locate or interpret the obligation to return confidential information, thus creating a structural flaw that contradicts the intent of the DTSA to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 U.S.C. § 1836, indicating civil proceedings related to trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall be from the date first above written until the earlier of (i) three (3) years after disclosure of Proprietary Information to Ciufo or (ii) written consent from Health Card that the Proprietary Information no longer needs to be treated as proprietary in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of the employment agreement shall be from the date first above written until the earlier of (i) three (3) years after disclosure of Proprietary Information to Ciufo or (ii) written consent from Health Card that the Proprietary Information no longer needs to be treated as proprietary in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Agreement' to 'employment agreement' in the context of this entire document, which is a Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement, introduces ambiguity and might cause disputes about its duration, especially considering that the contract itself has no relation to an employment agreement. In New York, ambiguity is construed against the drafter. This clause should be in a specific section discussing the agreement's term, not mixed in with definitions.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Altering the term clause to imply an 'employment agreement' where none exists introduces ambiguity. This could potentially invalidate the original confidentiality agreement because there's confusion as to what agreement the clause applies to.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'Agreement' to 'employment agreement' introduces ambiguity regarding the contract's scope, which could undermine its enforceability. New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 requires certain agreements to be in writing. By creating confusion about whether the confidentiality agreement is linked to an employment agreement, the altered text weakens the clarity of the original agreement's terms. The Perturbation Explanation states that the change might cause disputes about the agreement's duration and that ambiguity is construed against the drafter in New York, indicating a potential legal contradiction. Both scraped law snippets mention NY GOB § 5-701, highlighting the need for written agreements, but the change in the contract creates uncertainty as to which agreement is being referenced, thereby contradicting this law.

perturbation.original_text: Ciufo acknowledges and agrees that, in the event of any breach or threatened breach of any provision of this agreement, Health Card may be without an adequate remedy at law and, accordingly, shall be entitled to enforce such provision by temporary or permanent injunctive or mandatory relief obtained in an action or proceeding instituted in any court of competent jurisdiction without the necessity of proving damages and without prejudice to any other rights or remedies which it may have at law or in equity. Ciufo hereby agrees to the jurisdiction of all federal and state courts within the state of the defending party in connection with any matter relating to this Agreement. Ciufo further agrees that service of process may be made, in addition to all other methods permitted by law, by Certified Mail, Return Receipt Requested, sent to the address set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: Ciufo acknowledges and agrees that, in the event of any breach or threatened breach of any provision of this agreement related to newsletter subscriptions, Health Card may be without an adequate remedy at law and, accordingly, shall be entitled to enforce such provision by temporary or permanent injunctive or mandatory relief obtained in an action or proceeding instituted in any court of competent jurisdiction without the necessity of proving damages and without prejudice to any other rights or remedies which it may have at law or in equity. Ciufo hereby agrees to the jurisdiction of all federal and state courts within the state of the defending party in connection with any matter relating to this Agreement. Ciufo further agrees that service of process may be made, in addition to all other methods permitted by law, by Certified Mail, Return Receipt Requested, sent to the address set forth below.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the injunctive relief clause only to breaches related to 'newsletter subscriptions' significantly restricts Health Card's ability to protect its proprietary information through injunctive relief. It suggests that breaches related to other aspects of the agreement wouldn't be subject to this powerful remedy, undermining the agreement's enforceability under NY trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 6301 outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions in New York. This change restricts the ability to seek injunctive relief to a very narrow case. A court may be unwilling to grant an injunction under this agreement as written, especially regarding serious trade secret violations, because the injunctive relief clause is clearly very limited.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-09-26 2014-10-03 2015-01-16 2015-04-24 2015-07-10 2015-09-04 2015-10-09 2015-10-30 2015-11-27 2015-12-18 2015-12-25 2016-01-22 2016-07-01 2016-07-29 2016-08-05 2016-08-26 2016-12-02 2016-12-23 2017-04-28 2017-06-02 2017-08-04 2017-08-25 2017-10-27 2018-02-02 2018-04-20 2018-07-13 2018-08-03 2018-08-31 2019-01-04 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-02-15 2019-03-01 2019-04-19 2019-06-28 2019-07-12 2019-08-16 2019-08-30 2019-09-06 2019-09-20 2019-10-11 2019-10-18 2019-10-25 2019-11-01 2019-11-08 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2019-12-20 2019-12-27 2020-04-10 2020-04-17 2020-04-24 2020-06-19 2020-10-16 2020-11-13 2021-02-05 2021-04-02 2021-04-23 2021-05-14 2021-06-18 2021-07-30 2021-08-06 2021-08-13 2021-10-15 2021-11-12 2021-12-03 2021-12-10 2021-12-31 2022-01-07 2022-01-28 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-04-08 2022-05-06 2022-05-13 2022-05-27 2022-06-03 2022-06-10 2022-06-17 2022-07-08 2022-09-02 2022-10-07 2022-12-02 2022-12-16 2022-12-23 2023-01-06 2023-05-12 2023-06-30 2023-07-07 2023-09-22 2023-10-27 2023-11-03 2023-11-17 2023-12-01 2023-12-15 2024-01-05 2024-02-02 2024-02-16 2024-03-01 2024-03-08 2024-05-03 2024-05-17 2024-07-05 2024-09-06 2024-10-11 2024-11-29 2024-12-20 2024-12-27 2025-02-28 2025-03-14 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 6301 Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 63 § 6301. Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows Health Card to seek injunctive relief for any breach, while the changed text limits this relief only to breaches related to 'newsletter subscriptions.' This significantly restricts Health Card's ability to protect its rights under the agreement. NY C.P.L.R. § 6301, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows a preliminary injunction where a defendant threatens an act violating the plaintiff's rights, which would be injured during the action. By limiting injunctive relief, the changed text contradicts the broad scope of § 6301, as it makes it more difficult for Health Card to obtain an injunction for other breaches, effectively weakening its rights and remedies.
